---
title: "Illiterate America"
subtitle: "Why 50 years of effort have barely moved the needle on adult literacy"
author:
  - name: "[Authors TBD]"
    affiliation: Center for Global Development
date: "2026-04-20"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
    embed-resources: true
  pdf:
    toc: false
    fig-width: 10
    fig-height: 6
bibliography: references.bib
---

For the man who buys the newspaper and goes to the office everyday the crisis comes when his boss invites him for lunch to celebrate his performance. He is racked with anxiety. How will he go to the restaurant and order from a menu when he cannot read? His carefully orchestrated performance will be unmasked, his facade will crumble. Eventually, the man in Jonathan Kozol's shocking 1985 book, "Illiterate America," finds a solution because Howard Johnson has started putting pictures of the food in the menu. That simple act means that he can go to a fancier restaurant and still maintain his status.

When I tell my friends that story, they are shocked. And then comes the reaction: "Wow, I didn't realize that things were that bad in 1985–but they are so much better now." But here is the thing. America has had multiple "Wars on illiteracy" starting from the early 1920s when testing in the Armed Forces revealed that 24% of applicants were illiterate. And there *have* been massive gains, but virtually all the gains were concentrated between 1920 and 1950, when illiteracy declined from 13% of the population above the age of 10 to 3% (illiteracy here is defined as someone above the age of 10 who cannot read a sentence in any language). Between 1985 and 2017, functional illiteracy barely budged at around 20%. Between 2017 and 2023, things only became worse.

I want to stress three things.

The first is that you have to understand just how debilitating functional illiteracy is in a society that assumes that you can read and write. The 8% of U.S. adults who perform below Level 1 in PIAAC cannot figure out when they should take their medication given the instruction "Take one pill every morning" and cannot write their date of birth on a form that asks for it. The 13% of U.S. adults who perform at Level 1 cannot figure out when a library will close if the sign says that "The library will close on July 3rd at 5pm for independence day" and they cannot figure out how many calories in a package of 3 cookies if they are given the calories for each cookie. Put together a gathering of 100 randomly selected adult Americans in a restaurant, and 1 out of every 4 will not be able to read the menu. Things in Europe are better in numeracy, where "only" 25% fall below PIAAC level 1 compared to 34% in the United States, but similar for literacy (26% in Europe versus 28% in the U.S.).

The second is that the one place we can reliably predict the future is literacy. Studies that follow people over time show that functional literacy remains flat (or even declines) with age so that the 17-year-olds who cannot read a paragraph today will go through their adult lives functionally illiterate. Imagine that statement: In 2060, when we imagine that AI will have completely transformed society, one in four Americans older than 50 will still not be able to read a single line, interpret instructions or perform very basic mathematical calculations. Of course, it may not matter–technology enabled illiteracy may actually become a thing, as WhatsApp Voice notes and Tik Tok videos remind us–but, at least at some level, these are lives that fall short of their enormous potential through no choice of their own.

The third is that the dismal picture of 43 million illiterate adults and the lack of virtually any improvement for the last **50 years** is a double tragedy. It is a huge tragedy for the United States because of the enormous waste of talent that it represents. But it is also a tragedy for the rest of the world because the experience of the U.S., which in many ways has mimicked the experience of the rich Western European countries, is what feeds the narratives of the education elite in their summits and conventions. And with every failure, that narrative has turned increasingly negative: With the post-Covid declines in test scores, countries are now pulling back from funding education or even trying to improve test scores among children, particularly the poor.
